text: What does software engineering primarily deal with?
options: Systematic software development
Configuring computer network infrastructure
Managing enterprise database systems
Building and maintaining hardware systems
explanation: Software engineering is the systematic approach to the development, operation, and maintenance of software.

text: Who is considered the first person to use the term 'software engineering'?
options: Alan Turing
Edsger Dijkstra
Dennis Ritchie
Margaret Hamilton
explanation: Margaret Hamilton coined the term 'software engineering' during the Apollo space program.

text: Which of the following is NOT a characteristic of good software?
options: Dependability
Complexity
Maintainability
Efficiency
explanation: Complexity is not a desirable characteristic; good software should be simple and understandable.

text: What is the main goal of software engineering?
options: To incorporate the latest emerging technology
To minimize the overall development team size
To produce quality software within budget and schedule
To write source code as quickly as possible
explanation: The main goal is producing high-quality software that meets requirements within budget and schedule constraints.

text: Software engineering is a branch of which field?
options: Computer science and engineering
Electrical engineering and circuits
Mechanical engineering and design
Civil engineering and structures
explanation: Software engineering is a discipline within computer science and engineering.

text: What does the term 'software crisis' refer to?
options: Rapidly increasing hardware component costs
Insufficient programming language features
Problems in developing large-scale software
A critical shortage of trained programmers
explanation: The software crisis refers to difficulties encountered in developing large, complex software systems on time and within budget.

text: Which of the following is a software product?
options: Operating System
Mechanical keyboard
Monitor display unit
Laser printer device
explanation: An operating system is a software product; the others are hardware.

text: What is a software process?
options: A standalone computer program in the development
A relational database system within the project scope
A set of activities for software development
A physical hardware component during the software
explanation: A software process is a structured set of activities required to develop a software system.

text: Which attribute is essential for software quality?
options: Large file size
Complex algorithms
Reliability
Maximum features
explanation: Reliability, meaning the software performs correctly under stated conditions, is essential for quality.

text: What is the purpose of software documentation?
options: To increase overall project file size
To help understand and maintain the software
To make onboarding harder for new hires
To intentionally slow down the development
explanation: Documentation helps developers and users understand, use, and maintain the software.

text: Which IEEE standard defines software engineering?
options: IEEE 754
IEEE 610.12
IEEE 802.11
IEEE 1394
explanation: IEEE 610.12 provides the standard glossary of software engineering terminology.

text: What is the difference between software engineering and computer science?
options: CS primarily focuses on project management
They are fundamentally the same discipline
CS focuses on theory; SE focuses on practical development
SE primarily focuses on hardware architecture
explanation: Computer science focuses on theoretical foundations while software engineering focuses on practical software development.

text: Which of the following is a generic software process activity?
options: Software specification
Network setup
Hardware installation
Printing reports
explanation: Software specification is one of the four generic process activities along with development, validation, and evolution.

text: What is 'software reuse'?
options: Using existing software components in new systems
Writing all source code from scratch every time
Permanently deleting outdated legacy software
Copying proprietary code without any permission
explanation: Software reuse involves using existing software components or systems to build new software, reducing development time and cost.

text: Which factor contributed most to the software crisis?
options: Overly simple user requirements specifications
Rapidly advancing hardware computing capabilities
An oversaturation of qualified trained programmers
Increasing software complexity outpacing development methods
explanation: The software crisis was primarily caused by increasing complexity that existing development methods could not handle.

text: What is a software engineering method?
options: A specific type of programming language tool
A classification of computing hardware types
A structured approach for developing software
A specialized device for testing code logic
explanation: A software engineering method provides a structured approach including models, notations, and guidelines for development.

text: What is the significance of the NATO Conference 1968?
options: The initial public launch of the internet era for the development team
The initial development and creation of the UNIX system
The original invention of the C programming language throughout the project
First formal discussion of the software crisis and software engineering
explanation: The 1968 NATO Conference in Garmisch, Germany, formally addressed the software crisis and established software engineering as a discipline.

text: Which is NOT a type of software application?
options: Hardware software
System software
Web applications
Embedded software
explanation: Hardware software is not a recognized category; the others are valid types of software applications.

text: What does CASE stand for in software engineering?
options: Computer-Aided Software Engineering
Common Architecture System Engineering
Coded Application Standard Environment
Central Application Software Environment
explanation: CASE stands for Computer-Aided Software Engineering, referring to tools that support software development activities.

text: What is the role of a software engineer?
options: Writing source code exclusively and nothing else
Administering and managing databases exclusively
Designing, developing, testing, and maintaining software systems
Performing software testing activities exclusively
explanation: A software engineer is involved in all phases of software development, not just coding.

text: Which of the following best describes 'socio-technical systems'?
options: Systems that include both technical and organizational components
Systems that contain only technical components
Web-based social media communication platforms
Internal technical documentation management systems
explanation: Socio-technical systems include hardware, software, people, processes, and organizational structures working together.

text: What is Lehman's first law of software evolution?
options: Software systems must continually grow in size or become obsolete in the system context
The inherent complexity of software always decreases over its lifetime during implementation
Well-designed software systems never require updates or modifications at all for quality purposes
A program used in a real-world environment must continually change or become progressively less useful
explanation: Lehman's first law (Law of Continuing Change) states that software must adapt to its environment or become progressively less useful.

text: In the context of software engineering ethics, what does the ACM/IEEE Code of Ethics emphasize?
options: Public interest and professional responsibility
Rapid speed of software delivery timelines
Using the minimum documentation possible
Maximizing project profits above all else
explanation: The ACM/IEEE Code of Ethics emphasizes acting in public interest, maintaining integrity, and professional responsibility.

text: What is the key challenge addressed by Brooks' 'No Silver Bullet' paper?
options: Network bandwidth and throughput constraints by the organization
A critical lack of modern programming languages as a standard approach
Fundamental hardware performance limitations in practice typically
Essential complexity of software cannot be eliminated by any single technique
explanation: Brooks argued that no single technology or management technique can dramatically improve software productivity because of software's essential complexity.

text: Which concept distinguishes software engineering from ad hoc programming?
options: Systematic, disciplined, and quantifiable approach
Simply writing more lines of source code
Using a standard keyboard for input tasks
Utilizing more recent hardware component sets
explanation: Software engineering applies a systematic, disciplined, and quantifiable approach to software development, unlike ad hoc programming.

text: What is the 'wicked problem' concept in software engineering?
options: A straightforward and simple coding bug fix across all phases
A problem that is difficult to define and has no definitive solution
A software problem caused by a virus attack at every stage
A recurring hardware malfunction issue type in a systematic way
explanation: Wicked problems are complex problems where requirements are unclear, stakeholders disagree, and there is no single correct solution.

text: What is the primary difference between software engineering and systems engineering?
options: Software engineering also includes hardware design within the system boundary
Systems engineering exclusively covers testing by the development process
The two disciplines are entirely identical for the project goals according to best practices
Systems engineering covers the entire system including hardware; SE focuses on software
explanation: Systems engineering covers all aspects of computer-based systems development including hardware, while software engineering focuses specifically on software.

text: What does 'accidental complexity' mean in software engineering?
options: Unexpected failures in hardware components as defined by standards
Complexity introduced by the tools and methods used, not inherent to the problem
Random and unpredictable bugs in source code over the entire lifecycle
Inherent complexity from the problem domain and its related activities
explanation: Accidental complexity is introduced by development tools and approaches, as opposed to essential complexity inherent in the problem.

text: Which of the following represents a key principle of software engineering according to Sommerville?
options: Always maximize the total length of source code
Systems should be developed using a managed and understood process
Avoid writing any form of technical documentation
Use only one single programming language always
explanation: Sommerville emphasizes that software should be developed using a managed, understood development process appropriate to the software type.

text: What role does abstraction play in software engineering?
options: It increases the total length of the code
It eliminates the need for software testing
It helps manage complexity by hiding unnecessary details
It completely removes all project documentation
explanation: Abstraction is a fundamental principle that helps manage complexity by allowing developers to focus on relevant details while hiding unnecessary ones.

text: What is the Waterfall model?
options: A circular iterative development process
A random unstructured coding process
An agile methodology with sprint cycles
A linear sequential software development approach
explanation: The Waterfall model follows a linear sequential flow where each phase must be completed before the next begins.

text: Which phase comes first in the Waterfall model?
options: Implementation phase
Testing the system
Requirements analysis
Deployment to users
explanation: Requirements analysis is the first phase in the Waterfall model.

text: What is an iterative process model?
options: A model that omits all testing phases
A model that executes only a single run
A hardware-focused design model only
A model that repeats development cycles
explanation: An iterative process model repeats cycles of development, allowing refinement in each iteration.

text: What does the 'V-Model' emphasize?
options: Only source code implementation tasks
Hardware testing procedures and checks
Only requirements documentation writing
Verification and validation at each development stage
explanation: The V-Model pairs each development phase with a corresponding testing phase, emphasizing verification and validation.

text: In which model are requirements expected to be fully known at the start?
options: Spiral
Prototype
Waterfall
Agile
explanation: The Waterfall model assumes all requirements are fully known and documented before development begins.

text: What is a software development life cycle (SDLC)?
options: The process of planning, creating, testing, and deploying software
A physical computing hardware component unit in all development efforts
A specific type of programming language syntax for all project stakeholders
A specialized category of database system type within the given constraints
explanation: SDLC is the structured process that defines the stages involved in software development from inception to retirement.

text: Which model is also known as the 'Classic Life Cycle'?
options: Waterfall
RAD to achi
Spiral by
Agile and
explanation: The Waterfall model is also referred to as the Classic Life Cycle model.

text: What is prototyping in software development?
options: Building a preliminary version to explore requirements
Delivering the final production product in the engineering
Performing hardware acceptance testing as part of the methodology
Removing outdated legacy source code for effective project outcomes
explanation: Prototyping involves building an early version of the software to help clarify requirements and design.

text: Which process model involves risk analysis in each iteration?
options: RAD
Spiral
V-Model
Waterfall
explanation: The Spiral model explicitly includes risk analysis in each iteration cycle.

text: What is the main disadvantage of the Waterfall model?
options: Spending too much time on testing activities
Being excessively flexible and unstructured
Moving too fast through development phases
Difficult to accommodate changes after a phase is completed
explanation: The Waterfall model's rigid sequential nature makes it difficult to accommodate requirement changes once a phase is completed.

text: What are the four quadrants of the Spiral model?
options: Planning, Risk Analysis, Engineering, Evaluation
Gather, Analyze, Build, Ship within the project scope
Design, Code, Test, Review during the software lifecycle
Code, Test, Deploy, Maintain in the development process
explanation: The Spiral model's four quadrants are Planning, Risk Analysis, Engineering, and Customer Evaluation.

text: What is RAD (Rapid Application Development)?
options: An incremental model emphasizing short development cycles of 60-90 days
A standardized software testing framework tool for the development team
A formal documentation standard for projects throughout the project
A general-purpose programming language format in the system context
explanation: RAD emphasizes rapid prototyping and iterative delivery with very short development cycles.

text: Which model combines elements of the Waterfall and Iterative models?
options: Spiral model
Big Bang model
RAD
Incremental model
explanation: The Spiral model combines the systematic aspects of Waterfall with the iterative nature of prototyping.

text: What is the incremental model?
options: A model that skips the planning phase for quality purposes
Building the complete system all at once during implementation
A model used only for testing purposes in practice typically
Delivering the system in increments, each adding functionality
explanation: The incremental model delivers the system in increments, with each increment adding new functionality.

text: In the V-Model, what corresponds to the coding phase?
options: Integration testing
System testing
Unit testing
Acceptance testing
explanation: In the V-Model, unit testing corresponds to and validates the coding/implementation phase.

text: What is evolutionary development?
options: Developing software that remains unchanged permanently as a standard approach
Only creating project plans without writing code by the organization
Developing an initial version, exposing it to users, and refining through versions
Focusing solely on hardware evolution processes at every stage
explanation: Evolutionary development involves creating an initial implementation, getting user feedback, and evolving through multiple versions.

text: What is the main advantage of the incremental model?
options: The model requires absolutely no testing phase in a systematic way
Working software is produced early and feedback can be incorporated
No formal planning phase is ever required across all phases
It is always the fastest model available today for the project goals
explanation: The incremental model produces working software early, allowing users to provide feedback that can be incorporated in later increments.

text: What is a phase gate in process models?
options: An established coding standard for the team
A specific category of reported software bug
A review point at the end of each phase before proceeding
A physical entry gate for developer access
explanation: A phase gate is a review/decision point at the end of each phase where progress is evaluated before proceeding.

text: Which model is best suited for large, mission-critical projects?
options: Code and Fix
Big Bang accord
Spiral model
RAD within the
explanation: The Spiral model is well-suited for large, mission-critical projects due to its emphasis on risk management.

text: What is throwaway prototyping?
options: A category of finalized production product only and its related activities
Retaining and maintaining all prototypes built by the development process
A formal software testing methodology approach over the entire lifecycle
Building a prototype to understand requirements, then discarding it
explanation: Throwaway prototyping builds a quick prototype to clarify requirements, which is then discarded before building the actual system.

text: What is the Rational Unified Process (RUP)?
options: An iterative framework with four phases: Inception, Elaboration, Construction, Transition
A basic and simple coding technique only as defined by standards for all project stakeholders
A relational database design model type in all development efforts by the project team members
A specialized software testing tool suite within the given constraints
explanation: RUP is an iterative software development framework consisting of four phases with specific objectives and milestones.

text: What distinguishes the concurrent development model from other models?
options: It only supports a single developer at a time to achieve project objectives
All activities occur simultaneously with states rather than sequential phases
It completely eliminates all development phases and related components
It has absolutely no testing activities at all in the engineering discipline
explanation: The concurrent development model allows activities to exist in different states simultaneously rather than following a strict sequence.

text: In the Spiral model, what happens if a risk cannot be mitigated?
options: Skip directly to the deployment phase
Continue development regardless of the risk
Ignore the risk and move forward
The project may be terminated
explanation: If a significant risk cannot be mitigated in the Spiral model, the project may be terminated to avoid further losses.

text: What is the Personal Software Process (PSP)?
options: A hardware development process model type as part of the methodology
A standard network communication protocol in the development process
A team management and organizational method for effective project outcomes
A structured framework for individual developers to measure and improve their work
explanation: PSP is a structured framework developed by Watts Humphrey that helps individual software engineers measure, analyze, and improve their personal development process.

text: What is the Team Software Process (TSP)?
options: A method designed for a single developer only during the software lifecycle
A specific type of software testing process within the project scope
A hardware design and manufacturing method for the development team
A framework that builds on PSP to guide teams in developing software-intensive products
explanation: TSP extends PSP concepts to team-based software development, providing a framework for self-directed teams.

text: What does CMMI stand for and what is its purpose?
options: Capability Maturity Model Integration — a process improvement framework
Central Management Module Index for projects in the system context
Computer Made Machine Interface for hardware throughout the project
Code Maintenance Manual Integration standard during implementation
explanation: CMMI is a process improvement framework that provides organizations with essential elements of effective processes.

text: What are the five maturity levels of CMMI?
options: Initial, Managed, Defined, Quantitatively Managed, Optimizing
Code, Test, Deploy, Monitor, Maintain in practice typically
Beginner, Intermediate, Advanced, Expert, Master
Plan, Do, Check, Act, Review for quality purposes
explanation: CMMI has five maturity levels: Initial (1), Managed (2), Defined (3), Quantitatively Managed (4), and Optimizing (5).

text: What is the key difference between prescriptive and adaptive process models?
options: Prescriptive models do not involve any planning at every stage across all phases
Adaptive models typically require more documentation by the organization
There is absolutely no real difference between them as a standard approach
Prescriptive models define a fixed set of activities; adaptive models adjust based on feedback
explanation: Prescriptive models prescribe specific activities and ordering, while adaptive models adjust their process based on project feedback and changing requirements.

text: What is the Cleanroom software engineering approach?
options: A formal methods approach emphasizing defect prevention over defect removal
A hardware testing and validation environment according to best practices
A physical room dedicated to coding activities for the project goals
A model focused on clean code formatting style in a systematic way
explanation: Cleanroom software engineering uses formal methods and statistical quality certification to prevent defects rather than removing them through testing.

text: In the context of process models, what is a 'process pattern'?
options: A software design pattern for code modules within the system boundary
An individual test case for code validation and its related activities
A specific coding style guideline for teams by the development process
A template describing a proven process solution to a recurring problem
explanation: A process pattern is a template that describes a proven approach to a recurring process problem, including context, forces, and solution.

text: What is Agile software development?
options: A hardware design and manufacturing method as defined by standards
An iterative approach emphasizing flexibility and customer collaboration
A documentation-only waterfall approach for all project stakeholders
A strict sequential step-by-step approach over the entire lifecycle
explanation: Agile development is an iterative approach that emphasizes flexibility, customer collaboration, and rapid delivery of working software.

text: Which of the following is an Agile methodology?
options: Scrum
CMMI
V-Model
Waterfall
explanation: Scrum is one of the most popular Agile methodologies.

text: What is a 'Sprint' in Scrum?
options: A formal documentation writing phase only
A time-boxed iteration typically lasting 2-4 weeks
A physical running exercise for team health
A specific category of software defect type
explanation: A Sprint is a fixed-length iteration in Scrum, typically lasting 2-4 weeks, during which a potentially shippable product increment is created.

text: Who is the Product Owner in Scrum?
options: The quality assurance tester on the project in all development efforts
The person responsible for maximizing the value of the product
The database administrator managing storage by the project team members
The chief executive officer of the company within the given constraints
explanation: The Product Owner is responsible for maximizing product value and managing the Product Backlog.

text: What is the Agile Manifesto?
options: A declaration of four core values and twelve principles for Agile development
A programming language specification document to achieve project objectives
A legally binding contractual document only and related components
A hardware installation and setup manual in the engineering discipline
explanation: The Agile Manifesto, published in 2001, declares four core values and twelve principles that guide Agile software development.

text: How many values does the Agile Manifesto contain?
explanation: The Agile Manifesto contains four core values.

text: What does Scrum emphasize?
options: Hardware design and manufacturing work
Individual solo development practices only
Self-organizing teams and iterative progress
Producing extensive formal documentation
explanation: Scrum emphasizes self-organizing teams, iterative progress through sprints, and continuous improvement.

text: What is a 'User Story' in Agile?
options: A fictional novel written about end users for effective project outcomes
A comprehensive automated test case only in the development process
A formal detailed software bug report only as part of the methodology
A short description of a feature from the user's perspective
explanation: A User Story is a brief description of functionality from the end user's perspective.

text: What is a Daily Standup meeting?
options: A brief daily meeting where team members share progress and blockers
An extensive annual strategic planning event for the development team
A comprehensive weekly team meeting session during the software lifecycle
A detailed monthly project review session within the project scope
explanation: The Daily Standup is a brief 15-minute meeting where team members discuss what they did, what they will do, and any blockers.

text: Which Agile value prioritizes 'working software' over what?
options: Individual interactions
Responding to change
Customer collaboration
Comprehensive documentation
explanation: The Agile Manifesto values 'Working software over comprehensive documentation'.

text: What is the role of the Scrum Master?
options: Writing all of the source code for the team
Designing the relational database architecture
Managing the overall project budget allocations
Facilitating the Scrum process and removing impediments
explanation: The Scrum Master facilitates the Scrum process, removes impediments, and helps the team follow Scrum practices.

text: What is Extreme Programming (XP)?
options: A video game development methodology only throughout the project
An Agile methodology emphasizing engineering practices like pair programming and TDD
A project management scheduling tool only in the system context
A unit testing automation framework only during implementation for quality purposes
explanation: XP is an Agile methodology that emphasizes technical practices such as pair programming, test-driven development, and continuous integration.

text: What is a Product Backlog?
options: A finalized list of all completed work tasks
An ordered list of everything needed in the product
A comprehensive list of known bugs and issues
A physical hardware inventory listing sheet
explanation: The Product Backlog is a prioritized list of all desired work on the project, managed by the Product Owner.

text: What is the purpose of a Sprint Retrospective?
options: To reflect on the sprint and identify improvements
To plan all the tasks for the upcoming sprint
To demonstrate completed features to stakeholders
To write all required project documentation
explanation: The Sprint Retrospective allows the team to reflect on the past sprint and identify ways to improve their process.

text: What is Kanban in software development?
options: A visual workflow management method using boards and cards
A general-purpose programming language tool
A comprehensive testing automation framework
A relational database management system
explanation: Kanban is a visual method for managing work that uses boards and cards to visualize workflow and limit work in progress.

text: What is 'velocity' in Agile?
options: The amount of work a team can complete in a sprint
The physical typing speed of the developers
The network communication transfer bandwidth
The code compilation and build execution speed
explanation: Velocity measures the amount of work a team completes in a sprint, used for future planning.

text: What is pair programming?
options: Two programmers working together at one workstation
Two people using the same computer for different tasks
Two separate teams coding on different features
Programming in pairs of related source files
explanation: Pair programming is an XP practice where two developers work together at one workstation.

text: What is a Sprint Review?
options: A comprehensive team evaluation process event
A meeting to demonstrate completed work to stakeholders
A formal source code review session for the team
An individual performance review meeting session
explanation: The Sprint Review is a meeting at the end of each sprint where the team demonstrates completed work to stakeholders.

text: What is the 'Definition of Done' in Scrum?
options: A shared understanding of what it means for work to be complete
When the source code compiles without errors
When the project manager gives final approval
When all documentation has been written and reviewed
explanation: The Definition of Done is a shared agreement among the team about the criteria that must be met for a product increment to be considered complete.

text: What is Continuous Integration in Agile?
options: Integrating code once at the very end of the project in practice typically
Frequently merging code changes into a shared repository with automated testing
Performing only manual source code reviews periodically as a standard approach
Conducting annual system integration events for release by the organization
explanation: Continuous Integration involves frequently integrating code changes into a shared repository, with automated builds and tests.

text: What is the Scaled Agile Framework (SAFe)?
options: A framework for scaling Agile practices to enterprise level
A framework designed for individual small teams
A comprehensive testing automation framework tool
An established coding standard for all projects
explanation: SAFe is a framework for implementing Agile practices at enterprise scale.

text: What is the difference between Scrum and Kanban?
options: Scrum uses fixed-length sprints; Kanban uses continuous flow with WIP limits
Kanban uses fixed-length sprints; Scrum does not across all phases
The two methodologies are completely identical at every stage
Scrum has no defined roles; Kanban has defined roles in a systematic way
explanation: Scrum uses time-boxed sprints with defined roles, while Kanban uses continuous flow with work-in-progress limits.

text: What is 'technical debt' in Agile context?
options: Physical hardware equipment purchase expenses according to best practices
The cost of rework caused by choosing quick solutions over better approaches
Annual recurring software licensing fee costs within the system boundary
Financial money owed to the development team for the project goals
explanation: Technical debt refers to the implied cost of future rework caused by choosing a quick solution now instead of a better approach.

text: What is the Crystal methodology family?
options: A family of Agile methodologies sized by team size and criticality
A specialized software testing automation tool by the development process
A general-purpose programming language format and its related activities
A relational database management system design over the entire lifecycle
explanation: Crystal is a family of Agile methodologies created by Alistair Cockburn, with different versions based on team size and project criticality.

text: What is Feature-Driven Development (FDD)?
options: A random unstructured development approach only as defined by standards
A comprehensive documentation standard and format within the given constraints
An Agile method that organizes development around building features in two-week iterations
A formal software testing methodology and approach for all project stakeholders
explanation: FDD is an Agile method that focuses on designing and building features, using short iterations and model-driven development.

text: In Agile estimation, what is 'Planning Poker'?
options: A recreational team card game activity only in all development efforts
A project planning and scheduling tool suite by the project team members
A formal risk analysis assessment method type and related components
A consensus-based estimation technique using cards with numbers
explanation: Planning Poker is a consensus-based estimation technique where team members use numbered cards to estimate effort.

text: What is the purpose of a 'Burndown Chart'?
options: To track overall project financial expenses
To show remaining work versus time in a sprint or project
To measure and assess overall code quality
To track daily employee attendance records
explanation: A Burndown Chart graphically shows the amount of remaining work versus time.

text: What is Dynamic Systems Development Method (DSDM)?
options: An Agile framework that fixes time and cost, varying features to meet business needs
A standard network communication protocol type for effective project outcomes
A hardware design and manufacturing method only to achieve project objectives
A relational database design methodology approach in the engineering discipline
explanation: DSDM is an Agile framework that fixes time, cost, and quality at the outset, and adjusts features to deliver maximum business value.

text: What does 'MoSCoW' prioritization stand for?
options: Monitor, Observe, Scan, Check, Optimize, Watch
Must have, Should have, Could have, Won't have
A Russian city
Manage, Organize, Schedule, Control, Outline, Work
explanation: MoSCoW is a prioritization technique: Must have, Should have, Could have, and Won't have.

text: What is the concept of 'Emergent Design' in Agile?
options: Designing the entire system completely upfront first
Allowing the design to evolve incrementally as understanding grows
Having absolutely no design process at all ever
Copying designs from existing similar systems only
explanation: Emergent Design allows software design to evolve and improve over time through refactoring.

text: What is requirements engineering?
options: Performing software testing and validation in the development process
Designing hardware system architectures during the software lifecycle
Writing application source code for features as part of the methodology
The process of defining, documenting, and maintaining software requirements
explanation: Requirements engineering is the process of establishing what services the system should provide and the constraints under which it operates.

text: What is a functional requirement?
options: A constraint on the system design choices
A measurable performance goal for the system
A requirement that describes what the system should do
A detailed hardware specification document
explanation: Functional requirements describe specific functions or features that the system must perform.

text: What is a non-functional requirement?
options: A requirement that specifies quality attributes like performance or security
A requirement that is impossible to implement for the development team
A broken or flawed software requirement only within the project scope
A specification for hardware system components throughout the project
explanation: Non-functional requirements specify quality attributes such as performance, security, usability, and reliability.

text: What is requirements elicitation?
options: The process of gathering requirements from stakeholders
Performing systematic software testing runs
Writing application source code for modules
Deploying software to production servers
explanation: Requirements elicitation involves discovering and collecting requirements from stakeholders through various techniques.

text: Which of the following is a requirements elicitation technique?
options: Interview sessions
Debugging errors
Coding source logic
Code compilation
explanation: Interviews with stakeholders are a fundamental technique for gathering requirements.

text: What is a Software Requirements Specification (SRS)?
options: The application source code implementation
A comprehensive software test plan document
A complete end-user operational and help manual
A document describing all requirements of the system
explanation: An SRS is a comprehensive document that describes all functional and non-functional requirements of a software system.

text: Who are stakeholders in requirements engineering?
options: Only the programmers writing the source code
Only the paying customers of the software product
Only the project managers overseeing the work
Anyone who has an interest in or is affected by the system
explanation: Stakeholders include anyone who has an interest in the system.

text: What is the difference between user requirements and system requirements?
options: They are fundamentally the same concept entirely in the system context
User requirements are high-level; system requirements are detailed technical specifications
System requirements are always much simpler overall during implementation
User requirements are far more technically detailed for quality purposes
explanation: User requirements are high-level statements for customers, while system requirements are detailed specifications for developers.

text: What is requirements validation?
options: Implementing requirements directly into code by the organization
Initially writing new requirements documents in practice typically
Checking that requirements are correct, complete, and consistent
Permanently deleting unnecessary requirements as a standard approach
explanation: Requirements validation ensures that requirements accurately reflect stakeholder needs.

text: What is a use case?
options: A description of how a user interacts with the system to achieve a goal
A category of reported software defect type across all phases
A specific automated test case scenario only at every stage
A step in the deployment process workflow in a systematic way
explanation: A use case describes a sequence of interactions between a user and the system to accomplish a specific goal.

text: What is the difference between verification and validation in requirements?
options: Verification checks if we're building the product right; validation checks if we're building the right product
They are fundamentally identical concepts entirely for the project goals according to best practices
Verification is testing code; validation is writing code within the system boundary
Validation always occurs before verification ever does by the development process and its related activities
explanation: Verification ensures the product meets its specification, while validation ensures it meets user needs.

text: What is requirements traceability?
options: Tracking individual developer working hours over the entire lifecycle
The ability to trace each requirement through design, implementation, and testing
An established coding standard for the project for all project stakeholders
A specific debugging and troubleshooting technique as defined by standards
explanation: Requirements traceability links each requirement to its source and tracks it through all phases.

text: What is a use case diagram?
options: A process execution flowchart diagram only within the given constraints
A relational database schema diagram layout in all development efforts
A UML diagram showing actors and their interactions with the system
A network topology and routing diagram map by the project team members
explanation: A use case diagram is a UML diagram that shows relationships between actors and use cases.

text: What is requirements prioritization?
options: Permanently deleting all unimportant requirements
Ranking requirements by importance, risk, or business value
Grouping requirements by assigned developer team
Alphabetical ordering of all requirements listed
explanation: Requirements prioritization involves ranking requirements based on factors like business value, risk, and dependencies.

text: What is prototyping used for in requirements engineering?
options: Delivering the final production product to users
Performing application security penetration tests
Conducting system performance load testing runs
Helping stakeholders visualize and validate requirements
explanation: Prototyping helps stakeholders visualize the system and provides feedback to clarify requirements.

text: What is a scenario in requirements engineering?
options: A real-life example describing how a system might be used
An established coding guideline for the teams
A comprehensive deployment plan document only
A specific automated test execution script only
explanation: A scenario is a concrete example of system usage that helps clarify requirements.

text: What is requirements negotiation?
options: Rejecting all stakeholder proposed requirements
Resolving conflicts between stakeholders regarding requirements
Demanding that all requirements absolutely be met
Deliberately ignoring all stakeholder input
explanation: Requirements negotiation involves resolving conflicts and reaching agreements among stakeholders.

text: What is domain analysis in requirements engineering?
options: Studying specific programming language syntax in the engineering discipline
Studying the application domain to understand its concepts and constraints
Analyzing the database schema domain structure and related components
Analyzing network domain name configurations to achieve project objectives
explanation: Domain analysis involves studying the application area to understand its terminology, concepts, and constraints.

text: What is the IEEE 830 standard?
options: A networking communication protocol standard
A standard for writing software requirements specifications
A software testing methodology and standard
A hardware design and manufacturing standard
explanation: IEEE 830 provides recommended practices for writing software requirements specifications.

text: What is a constraint in requirements engineering?
options: A defect discovered in the source code base for effective project outcomes
A restriction or limitation on the system or development process
A programming language built-in feature set in the development process
A specific type of software test case only as part of the methodology
explanation: A constraint is a restriction that limits design choices.

text: What is the Volere requirements template?
options: A software testing automation template setup within the project scope
A comprehensive template for organizing and documenting requirements with quality gateways
A deployment configuration setup template for the development team throughout the project
A source code implementation template file during the software lifecycle
explanation: The Volere template provides a structured approach to requirements gathering and documentation.

text: What is goal-oriented requirements engineering?
options: An approach where requirements are derived from stakeholder and system goals
Setting performance and speed benchmarks for quality purposes
Setting rigid project deadline target dates in the system context
Writing only goals instead of actual code during implementation
explanation: Goal-oriented RE derives requirements from high-level stakeholder goals.

text: What is the KAOS methodology?
options: A deployment and release methodology guide by the organization
A goal-oriented requirements engineering methodology using formal methods
A specific software testing methodology only as a standard approach
A random unstructured development approach in practice typically
explanation: KAOS is a goal-oriented methodology that uses formal methods to derive requirements from goals.

text: What is requirements volatility?
options: Requirements that remain perfectly stable always at every stage
A type of volatile computer memory hardware across all phases
A recurring issue with hardware component sets in a systematic way
The tendency of requirements to change during the development process
explanation: Requirements volatility refers to how frequently requirements change.

text: What is viewpoint-oriented requirements engineering?
options: Collecting requirements from multiple stakeholder viewpoints to ensure completeness
Only considering developer technical viewpoints within the system boundary
Deliberately ignoring all stakeholder views given according to best practices
Using only a single stakeholder perspective view for the project goals
explanation: Viewpoint-oriented RE collects requirements from all relevant perspectives.

text: What is the difference between 'shall' and 'should' in requirements documents?
options: 'Shall' indicates mandatory requirements; 'should' indicates desirable but optional
The two terms mean exactly the same thing always by the development process
Neither term is used in requirements documents ever over the entire lifecycle
'Should' is mandatory and 'shall' is always optional and its related activities
explanation: In requirements documents, 'shall' denotes mandatory requirements, while 'should' indicates desirable features.

text: What is formal specification in requirements engineering?
options: Drawing informal pictures of system requirements
Using mathematical notation to precisely define system requirements
Using programming code as the requirements source
Writing requirements in an informal manner only
explanation: Formal specification uses mathematical notation to precisely and unambiguously define system requirements.

text: What are cross-cutting concerns in requirements?
options: Requirements that are very easy to implement fast as defined by standards
Requirements that are considered not important for all project stakeholders
Requirements that affect multiple modules and cannot be cleanly decomposed
Requirements from only one single stakeholder within the given constraints
explanation: Cross-cutting concerns are requirements like security and logging that span multiple modules.

text: What is the i* (i-star) framework?
options: A numerical system for rating software products in all development efforts
A goal-oriented and agent-oriented modeling framework for requirements engineering
A general-purpose application programming framework by the project team members
A comprehensive software testing automation framework and related components
explanation: The i* framework is used for modeling stakeholder goals, dependencies, and relationships.

text: What is the difference between derived and imposed requirements?
options: Derived requirements are always considered optional in the development process during the software lifecycle
Derived requirements come from higher-level requirements; imposed requirements come from external constraints
They are fundamentally the same concept entirely to achieve project objectives in the engineering discipline
Imposed requirements are always derived from code for effective project outcomes as part of the methodology
explanation: Derived requirements come from higher-level requirements through analysis, while imposed requirements come from external sources.

text: What is software project management?
options: Planning, organizing, and controlling software development activities
Designing hardware system architectures throughout the project
Writing application source code and modules within the project scope
Performing software testing and debugging for the development team
explanation: Software project management involves planning, organizing, monitoring, and controlling all aspects of software development.

text: What is a Gantt chart?
options: A bar chart showing project schedule and task dependencies
A relational database schema design diagram
A specific type of source code format only
A specialized software testing automation tool
explanation: A Gantt chart is a horizontal bar chart that illustrates a project schedule.

text: What does WBS stand for?
options: Work Based Standards
Web Browser Software
Work Breakdown Structure
Wireless Broadcasting System
explanation: WBS is a hierarchical decomposition of project work into smaller, manageable components.

text: What is a project milestone?
options: An established coding standard and guideline
A significant point or event in a project timeline
A physical unit of measured distance only
A specific category of software defect type
explanation: A milestone marks the completion of a major deliverable or phase.

text: What is the critical path in project management?
options: The longest sequence of dependent tasks determining the minimum project duration
The path with the fewest total listed tasks for quality purposes
The shortest possible project path for tasks during implementation
The set of easiest tasks in the project plan in the system context
explanation: The critical path determines the minimum time needed to complete the project.

text: What is project scope?
options: The total number of developers on the team as a standard approach
The total size of the source code base only in practice typically
The defined boundaries of what the project will and will not deliver
Only the financial budget for the project by the organization
explanation: Project scope defines what will and will not be delivered.

text: What is a deliverable in project management?
options: A tangible or intangible output produced as a result of project work
A scheduled meeting agenda and minutes record in a systematic way
A food delivery order to the project team at every stage
A formal software defect bug report document across all phases
explanation: A deliverable is any measurable output that must be produced to complete the project.

text: What is project estimation?
options: Measuring physical hardware performance speeds
Predicting the effort, cost, and duration needed for a project
Simply counting total lines of application code
Making random guesses without any real basis
explanation: Project estimation involves predicting resources, effort, cost, and time required.

text: What is resource allocation?
options: Deleting unnecessary project data and files
Assigning available resources to project tasks
Writing comprehensive project documentation
Purchasing new computing hardware equipment
explanation: Resource allocation assigns and manages resources to project tasks.

text: What are the three constraints of project management?
options: Design, Build, Ship within the system boundary
Code, Test, Deploy for the project goals
Scope, Time, Cost (the triple constraint)
Plan, Do, Check according to best practices
explanation: The triple constraint consists of scope, time, and cost.

text: What is the COCOMO model?
options: A comprehensive software testing methodology and its related activities
A creational object-oriented design pattern over the entire lifecycle
A popular chocolate confectionery brand name by the development process
A model for estimating software development effort based on lines of code
explanation: COCOMO estimates software development effort, cost, and schedule based on software size.

text: What is function point analysis?
options: A method for measuring software size based on functionality delivered to users
A specific software testing automation technique for all project stakeholders
A systematic debugging and tracing methodology within the given constraints
Counting total functions in application code as defined by standards
explanation: Function Point Analysis measures software size based on functionality provided to users.

text: What is Earned Value Management (EVM)?
options: A corporate employee salary payment system in all development efforts
A technique for measuring project performance by comparing planned vs actual progress
An established coding standard and guideline set and related components
A specific software testing coverage metric only by the project team members
explanation: EVM integrates scope, schedule, and cost measurements to assess project performance.

text: What is the difference between PERT and CPM?
options: PERT does not produce any chart output at all for effective project outcomes
The two techniques are completely identical overall to achieve project objectives
CPM is exclusively used for software projects in the engineering discipline
PERT uses probabilistic time estimates; CPM uses deterministic time estimates
explanation: PERT uses three time estimates while CPM uses a single deterministic estimate.

text: What is slack (float) in project scheduling?
options: A specific category of software defect type during the software lifecycle
Additional unnecessary source code that was written in the development process
Being unproductive and lazy at work always as part of the methodology
The amount of time a task can be delayed without delaying the project
explanation: Slack is the amount of time a non-critical task can be delayed without affecting project completion.

text: What is a project baseline?
options: The very first line of application source code for the development team
An approved version of the project plan used to measure performance
A performance testing benchmark result value throughout the project
The initial starting point for all coding work within the project scope
explanation: A project baseline is an approved snapshot used as a reference for measuring performance.

text: What is the purpose of a project charter?
options: To write application source code for features in the system context
To perform software testing and validation runs during implementation
To formally authorize the project and define its objectives and stakeholders
To deploy software to production server systems for quality purposes
explanation: A project charter formally authorizes the project and defines objectives and stakeholders.

text: What is the difference between effort and duration in project estimation?
options: Effort is total person-hours; duration is calendar time
The two terms are exactly the same thing always
Effort is always measured in monetary currency
Duration is always longer than effort estimates
explanation: Effort is total person-hours of work; duration is calendar time from start to finish.

text: What is a risk in project management?
options: An uncertain event that could positively or negatively affect the project
An absolute certainty in the project plan only in practice typically
A documented project functional requirement by the organization
A task that has already been fully completed as a standard approach
explanation: A risk is an uncertain event that could affect project objectives.

text: What is the purpose of a status report?
options: To design relational database architectures in a systematic way
To write application source code for features at every stage
To compile and build software executable files across all phases
To communicate project progress, issues, and plans to stakeholders
explanation: Status reports communicate project progress and issues to stakeholders.

text: What is the COCOMO II model's key improvement over COCOMO I?
options: It uses significantly simpler mathematical formulas within the system boundary
It provides much faster calculation processing speed for the project goals
It produces much better visual graphics output according to best practices
It accounts for modern development practices like reuse and object-oriented development
explanation: COCOMO II handles modern practices including software reuse and object-oriented development.

text: What is the Delphi estimation technique?
options: A specific programming implementation technique only
A comprehensive software testing methodology type
A production deployment automation technique type
An expert-based estimation method using anonymous iterative rounds
explanation: The Delphi technique uses multiple rounds of anonymous expert estimation to converge on a consensus.

text: What is the Putnam model (SLIM)?
options: A personal health and diet improvement plan only by the development process
A comprehensive software testing process model type and its related activities
A software architecture and design model for teams over the entire lifecycle
A software estimation model relating effort to delivery time using the Rayleigh curve
explanation: The Putnam model uses the Rayleigh curve to model the relationship between effort, time, and software size.

text: What is the difference between bottom-up and top-down estimation?
options: Top-down estimation is always much more accurate in all development efforts by the project team members
Bottom-up estimation is always significantly faster within the given constraints
Bottom-up estimates individual tasks then aggregates; top-down estimates the whole project then decomposes
Both approaches always give identical final results as defined by standards for all project stakeholders
explanation: Bottom-up starts with individual estimates and aggregates; top-down starts with overall estimates and decomposes.

text: What is the concept of 'Brooks' Law'?
options: Adding people to a late software project makes it later
A specific programming coding law and its rules
Established laws about code formatting standards
More developers always mean faster project work
explanation: Brooks' Law states that adding manpower to a late project makes it even later.

text: What is the Schedule Performance Index (SPI) in EVM?
options: A comprehensive software testing progress index in the engineering discipline
A specific source code quality coding metric only to achieve project objectives
The ratio of earned value to planned value, indicating schedule efficiency
A generic system processing speed measurement and related components
explanation: SPI = EV/PV; less than 1 means behind schedule; greater than 1 means ahead.

text: What is the Cost Performance Index (CPI) in EVM?
options: A general consumer price comparison index only for effective project outcomes
A specific source code quality coding metric only as part of the methodology
The ratio of earned value to actual cost, indicating cost efficiency
A hardware system performance rating metric in the development process
explanation: CPI = EV/AC; less than 1 means over budget; greater than 1 means under budget.

text: What is Monte Carlo simulation used for in project management?
options: A recreational casino gambling game activity only during the software lifecycle
Simulating project outcomes using probability distributions to assess risk
A specific software coding implementation technique within the project scope
A software architecture and design method guide for the development team
explanation: Monte Carlo simulation runs multiple scenarios to assess the likelihood of different project outcomes.

text: What is the concept of 'crashing' in project scheduling?
options: An unplanned system failure or complete breakdown
Adding resources to critical path tasks to reduce project duration
Permanently removing tasks from the project plan
Completely cancelling the entire project scope
explanation: Crashing adds resources to critical path activities to reduce their duration.

text: What is fast tracking in project management?
options: Reducing the total number of project requirements during implementation
Requiring the team to work overtime every day throughout the project
Skipping all software testing activities entirely in the system context
Performing sequential activities in parallel to compress the schedule
explanation: Fast tracking performs normally sequential activities in parallel to compress the schedule.

text: What is software design?
options: Writing detailed end-user operation manual docs as a standard approach
Deploying software to production server systems in practice typically
Writing comprehensive test case scenario suites for quality purposes
The process of defining the architecture, components, and interfaces of a system
explanation: Software design defines software architecture, components, interfaces, and other characteristics.

text: What is modularity in software design?
options: Using only one file for all the source code
Dividing a system into separate, independent modules
Writing one single large monolithic program
Avoiding the use of functions in the code
explanation: Modularity divides a system into distinct modules that can be developed and maintained independently.

text: What is cohesion in software design?
options: Unnecessary duplication of source code and logic
Multiple modules depending on each other heavily
A specific category of software defect and bug
The degree to which elements within a module belong together
explanation: Cohesion measures how strongly related the responsibilities of a single module are.

text: What is coupling in software design?
options: A high degree of internal module cohesion only
A specific type of data structure or element
The degree of interdependence between modules
A programming language built-in feature type
explanation: Coupling measures how much one module depends on another.

text: Which is preferred: high cohesion or low cohesion?
options: No cohesion at all
Both are equally good
Low cohesion is preferred
High cohesion is preferred
explanation: High cohesion means the module's elements are strongly related and focused.

text: Which is preferred: tight coupling or loose coupling?
options: Loose coupling is preferred
No coupling at all works
Tight coupling is preferred
Both are equally good
explanation: Loose coupling reduces dependencies between modules, making the system more maintainable.

text: What is a design pattern?
options: A specific coding style and convention set
A user interface visual color scheme choice
A reusable solution to a commonly occurring design problem
A comprehensive software testing method type
explanation: A design pattern is a general, reusable solution to a commonly occurring problem.

text: What is abstraction in design?
options: A specific type of runtime software error type
Adding increasingly more implementation detail
Removing all application source code and files
Hiding complex details and showing only essential features
explanation: Abstraction simplifies complex systems by hiding implementation details.

text: What is information hiding?
options: Concealing internal design decisions within a module from other modules
Permanently deleting stored information across all phases
Hiding files on the operating system disk at every stage
Encrypting sensitive data during transit by the organization
explanation: Information hiding restricts access to module internals through well-defined interfaces.

text: What is the difference between high-level and low-level design?
options: They are fundamentally the same thing exactly in a systematic way
High-level defines architecture; low-level defines detailed component design
Low-level design is always more important overall for the project goals
High-level design is done after coding is finished according to best practices
explanation: High-level design defines overall architecture; low-level design specifies detailed component designs.

text: What is the Singleton design pattern?
options: A pattern that ensures a class has only one instance with a global access point
A comprehensive software testing design pattern by the development process
A relational database schema design pattern type and its related activities
A pattern designed for creating multiple instances within the system boundary
explanation: The Singleton pattern restricts a class to a single instance with a global access point.

text: What is the Observer design pattern?
options: A pattern where objects are notified of state changes in another object
A creational object instantiation design pattern as defined by standards
A structural composition and wrapping pattern for all project stakeholders
A system security and access control pattern over the entire lifecycle
explanation: The Observer pattern defines a one-to-many dependency where dependents are notified of state changes.

text: What is the Factory Method pattern?
options: A pattern that defines an interface for creating objects, letting subclasses decide which class to instantiate
A specialized debugging and tracing tool suite to achieve project objectives in the engineering discipline
A comprehensive software testing method and tool by the project team members and related components
A physical manufacturing production process flow within the given constraints in all development efforts
explanation: The Factory Method lets subclasses decide which class to instantiate.

text: What is the MVC design pattern?
options: A comprehensive software testing pattern type as part of the methodology
A specific type of program variable declaration for effective project outcomes
Model-View-Controller, separating data, presentation, and control logic
A software deployment automation pattern type in the development process
explanation: MVC separates an application into Model, View, and Controller components.

text: What is the Strategy design pattern?
options: A specific software testing strategy and plan within the project scope
A project management planning approach method during the software lifecycle
A pattern that defines a family of algorithms and makes them interchangeable
A software deployment automation strategy type for the development team
explanation: The Strategy pattern defines a family of algorithms and makes them interchangeable at runtime.

text: What are the three categories of GoF design patterns?
options: Input, Process, Output in the system
Creational, Structural, Behavioral
Design, Code, Test during implementation
Easy, Medium, Hard throughout the project
explanation: The Gang of Four classified patterns into Creational, Structural, and Behavioral categories.

text: What is the Open/Closed Principle?
options: Software entities should be open for extension but closed for modification
Never closing running desktop applications ever as a standard approach
Always keeping file handles open in the code in practice typically
Using only open source software for projects for quality purposes
explanation: The Open/Closed Principle states that entities should be extensible without modifying existing code.

text: What is the Liskov Substitution Principle?
options: A specific naming convention for all variables by the organization at every stage
A comprehensive software testing principle type across all phases in a systematic way
A relational database normalization principle for the project goals according to best practices
Objects of a superclass should be replaceable with objects of a subclass without affecting correctness
explanation: LSP states that derived classes must be substitutable for their base classes.

text: What is the DRY principle?
options: Keep code dry and clean within the system boundary
Delete Redundant Yields by the development process
Don't Repeat Yourself — avoid code duplication
Develop Robust Yields and its related activities
explanation: DRY states that every piece of knowledge should have a single representation.

text: What is a class diagram?
options: A project timeline for all project stakeholders within the given constraints
A network diagram in all development efforts by the project team members
A classroom layout over the entire lifecycle as defined by standards
A UML diagram showing classes, attributes, methods, and relationships
explanation: A class diagram shows classes with their attributes, operations, and relationships.

text: What is the SOLID acronym in object-oriented design?
options: Single Responsibility, Open/Closed, Liskov Substitution, Interface Segregation, Dependency Inversion
A comprehensive software testing framework set for effective project outcomes
A specific general-purpose coding language type in the engineering discipline
A classification of physical building materials and related components to achieve project objectives
explanation: SOLID represents five principles of object-oriented design.

text: What is the Dependency Inversion Principle?
options: All dependencies should always point downward only as part of the methodology
All module dependencies should be completely removed in the development process
High-level modules should not depend on low-level modules; both should depend on abstractions
Dependencies between modules should be circular during the software lifecycle
explanation: DIP states that both high-level and low-level modules should depend on abstractions.

text: What is the Interface Segregation Principle?
options: Avoid defining all interfaces in the system entirely
Use one single large monolithic interface always
Clients should not be forced to depend on interfaces they don't use
Combine all interfaces into a single large one
explanation: ISP states that no client should be forced to depend on methods it does not use.

text: What is the Decorator design pattern?
options: A pattern that attaches additional responsibilities to an object dynamically
A user interface visual styling pattern only within the project scope
A relational database schema design pattern type for the development team
A comprehensive software testing design pattern throughout the project
explanation: The Decorator pattern dynamically adds responsibilities to an object by wrapping it.

text: What is the difference between composition and inheritance?
options: The two concepts are completely identical always in the system context
Inheritance is never used in modern source code for quality purposes
Composition is always the wrong approach to use during implementation
Inheritance creates an 'is-a' relationship; composition creates a 'has-a' relationship
explanation: Inheritance defines 'is-a' relationships; composition defines 'has-a' relationships.

text: What is the Adapter design pattern?
options: A comprehensive software testing adapter tool
A relational database connection driver and tool
A physical electrical power adapter device unit
A pattern that allows incompatible interfaces to work together
explanation: The Adapter pattern converts one interface into another that clients expect.

text: What is the Command design pattern?
options: A pattern that encapsulates a request as an object, allowing parameterization and queuing
A production deployment automation command set by the organization at every stage
A command-line interface terminal command only in practice typically
A comprehensive software testing command suite as a standard approach
explanation: The Command pattern encapsulates a request as an object for parameterization and queuing.

text: What is the Template Method pattern?
options: A source code boilerplate template file format across all phases in a systematic way
A software testing test case plan template set according to best practices
A comprehensive project documentation template for the project goals
A pattern that defines the skeleton of an algorithm, deferring some steps to subclasses
explanation: The Template Method defines the skeleton of an algorithm, letting subclasses override specific steps.

text: What is the concept of 'design debt'?
options: Financial debt for purchasing design tools only within the system boundary
The accumulated cost of design shortcuts that will require future rework
A specific type of design pattern category name by the development process
A concept related only to software testing work and its related activities
explanation: Design debt accumulates when design shortcuts are taken, leading to increased maintenance costs.

text: What is the difference between architectural design and detailed design?
options: They are fundamentally identical concepts entirely over the entire lifecycle as defined by standards
Architectural design only involves UI layout work in all development efforts by the project team members
Detailed design always comes before architectural for all project stakeholders within the given constraints
Architectural design defines system structure; detailed design specifies algorithms and data structures within components
explanation: Architectural design establishes overall structure; detailed design specifies internal component workings.

text: What is software architecture?
options: The high-level structure of a software system defining its components and their relationships
A specific type of programming language syntax in the engineering discipline
A comprehensive software testing framework type for effective project outcomes
The actual application source code itself only and related components to achieve project objectives
explanation: Software architecture is the high-level structure defining components and their relationships.

text: What is a client-server architecture?
options: A standalone desktop application design only during the software lifecycle
An architecture where clients request services from a central server
A decentralized peer-to-peer network system in the development process
A single standalone computer system by itself as part of the methodology
explanation: Client-server architecture divides the system into clients and servers.

text: What is a layered architecture?
options: An architecture organized into horizontal layers, each providing services to the layer above
A completely flat system structure layout design within the project scope
A random unorganized structure for the code throughout the project in the system context
A circular system structure design layout type for the development team
explanation: Layered architecture organizes the system into layers where each provides services to the layer above.

text: What is a monolithic architecture?
options: A client-server communication system setup only in practice typically
A single-tiered application where all components are tightly integrated
A fully distributed computing system setup design during implementation
A microservices-based architecture approach type for quality purposes
explanation: A monolithic architecture is a single, unified application with interconnected components.

text: What is the purpose of software architecture?
options: To write application source code much faster as a standard approach
To provide a blueprint for the system and enable communication among stakeholders
To completely eliminate the software testing phase by the organization
To reduce the overall project development team at every stage
explanation: Software architecture serves as a blueprint and facilitates communication among stakeholders.

text: What is a three-tier architecture?
options: A three-story office building design layout across all phases
An architecture with presentation, business logic, and data tiers
Three different programming languages used for the project goals
A development team of three programmers in a systematic way
explanation: Three-tier architecture separates presentation, business logic, and data tiers.

text: What is a repository architecture?
options: A network infrastructure architecture type only by the development process
A Git source code version control repository according to best practices
An architecture where components share data through a central data store
An operating system file storage system only within the system boundary
explanation: Repository architecture uses a central data store shared by all components.

text: What is a pipe-and-filter architecture?
options: An access security management system type only as defined by standards
A physical plumbing infrastructure system design and its related activities
A network traffic filtering system setup only over the entire lifecycle
An architecture where data flows through a sequence of processing components
explanation: Pipe-and-filter processes data through a series of filters connected by pipes.

text: What is an event-driven architecture?
options: An architecture where components communicate through events
A sequential processing architecture style only
A scheduling calendar management system only
A batch data processing system design only
explanation: Event-driven architecture uses events to trigger communication between components.

text: What is a distributed system architecture?
options: An architecture where components run on multiple networked computers
A standalone desktop application design approach within the given constraints
A single standalone computer hardware unit only for all project stakeholders
A local desktop application installation setup in all development efforts
explanation: Distributed systems spread components across multiple networked computers.

text: What is microservices architecture?
options: An architecture where the application is composed of small, independent services
A traditional monolithic design approach to apps and related components
A single centralized database system approach to achieve project objectives
One single large monolithic service application by the project team members
explanation: Microservices structures an application as a collection of small, independent services.

text: What is the difference between SOA and microservices?
options: SOA is a much newer architectural approach style as part of the methodology
SOA uses enterprise service bus for communication; microservices use lightweight protocols
Microservices are generally larger in scope overall for effective project outcomes
The two approaches are completely identical overall in the engineering discipline
explanation: SOA uses centralized ESB; microservices favor lightweight communication.

text: What is an architectural style?
options: A named collection of architectural design decisions applicable to recurring design problems
A user interface visual style and theme design during the software lifecycle
A specific coding style and convention guideline in the development process
A project documentation format and standard set within the project scope
explanation: An architectural style is a set of design decisions that give a system its distinctive character.

text: What is the Model-View-ViewModel (MVVM) pattern?
options: An architectural pattern separating UI, presentation logic, and business logic/data
A relational database normalization pattern type for the development team
A comprehensive software testing pattern design throughout the project
A production deployment automation pattern type in the system context
explanation: MVVM separates View, ViewModel, and Model components.

text: What are architectural views?
options: Database views defined with SQL query language for quality purposes
User interface screens and layout page designs in practice typically
Different perspectives of a system architecture for different stakeholders
Screen views displayed to the end users only during implementation
